keyPoints: Retrieval-practice after LLM-guided study improves transfer when prompts require reconstruction or problem solving that align with the target task (TAP).
Design prompts using modular structure (context, question, format, examples) to mirror final-task demands and support informative feedback.
Incorporate near-transfer and far-transfer probes and measure calibration (confidence-accuracy) to map learning across contexts.
Use spaced retrieval and delayed testing to boost durability and transfer in AI-assisted learning.
Embed deliberate difficulty, diverse prompts, and constraint-based workflows to guard against homogenization and over-reliance.
Adopt rigorous research design (randomized/quasi-experimental, adequate power, pre-registration) with moderation analyses for initial retrieval success, prior knowledge, and task complexity.
Provide content with meaningful principles and scaffolds; ensure AI prompts avoid hallucinations and stay aligned with learning objectives.
